Mississippi River Barge Freight Forecast Model

Predicting harvest-season downbound grain rates three months in advance

{# ===================================================== #}

1. Model Overview

This report documents a forecasting model for monthly average Mississippi River barge freight rates during harvest months (September, October, November), predicted approximately three months in advance using data available in June, July, and August. The benchmark is the USDA three-month forward barge rate quote — a market-consensus forecast embedding grain trader expectations about supply, demand, and river conditions.

Approach

The forecasting model is a regularised linear regression (ElasticNetCV) trained on log-transformed rates with walk-forward validation — train on all years before the test year, test on the next year, expanding the training window each iteration. The model uses the forward rate itself as a feature, which allows it to learn systematic biases in the market's own forecast.

Features (42 variables, 25 retained after L1 regularisation)

  • Forward rate — market consensus forecast
  • Forward-spot spread — implied rate change
  • Spot rate 4-wk / 8-wk averages
  • Rate momentum — 4-wk minus 8-wk
  • Last year's harvest rate — seasonal reference
  • Climatological average — long-term mean
  • YTD rate average + YoY change
  • Thebes gauge: level, min, trend, departure
  • Baton Rouge and St. Louis gauge heights
  • Memphis discharge (cfs)
  • Gauge spread St. Louis minus Thebes
  • Composite drought indicator (4-gauge)
  • Low-water crisis flag
  • Midwest diesel price ($/gal)
  • USD/BRL + month-over-month change
  • Shuttle train and truck cost indices
  • Gulf export inspections + trend
  • Soybean futures price
  • Corn and soy production estimates
  • Soy planting progress
  • Harvest month, origin month, origin dummies
{# ===================================================== #}

2. Data Sources

Nine independent data pipelines feed the model, all re-runnable from the src/ingest/ scripts. Data is stored in a local DuckDB database with raw Parquet caches for re-processing.

Pipeline Architecture

Each data source has a dedicated ingest module that fetches from the upstream API, caches raw responses to Parquet, and upserts into DuckDB using stable primary keys. Re-running any script is idempotent.

APIs requiring keys (EIA, FRED, NASS) load credentials from .env. Free APIs (USDA AMS Socrata, USGS NWIS) need no authentication.

Table 2-1 Data Pipelines

DatasetSourceAPIDetailRowsCoverage
Barge Rates (target)USDA AMSagtransport.usda.govdeqi-uken8,1342004–2026
3-Month Forward RatesUSDA AMSagtransport.usda.govuuhv-5etw8,1342004–2026
River Gauge DataUSGS NWISwaterservices.usgs.gov8 sites108,4102000–2026
Export InspectionsUSDA AMSagtransport.usda.govsruw-w49i32,3331983–2026
Diesel PricesEIAapi.eia.govPADD 2 & 33,3481994–2026
Exchange RatesFREDapi.stlouisfed.orgDTWEXBGS, DEXBZUS11,6692000–2026
Crop ProgressUSDA NASSquickstats.nass.usda.govCorn & Soy9,3502000–2026
Production EstimatesUSDA NASSquickstats.nass.usda.govWASDE proxy1602000–2025
Cost IndicatorsUSDA AMSagtransport.usda.gov8uye-ieij1,2342002–2026

Total: 182,772 rows across 9 tables.

{# ===================================================== #}

3. Exploratory Data Analysis

3.1 Barge Rates: 22 Years of Weekly Data

Weekly downbound grain barge rates as a percentage of the 1976 Waterway Freight Bureau Tariff No. 7. Rates above 100% indicate spot rates exceeding the benchmark tariff.

Figure 3-1 Historical Barge Rates by Origin

The 2022 spike (2,800% at Cairo-Memphis) dwarfs all other variation — driven by historic low water on the lower Mississippi. The seven origins move broadly together, with persistent spreads reflecting tow distance to the Gulf.

3.2 Seasonality & Correlation Structure

Figure 3-2 Average Seasonal Pattern

Rates trough in June–July and peak in September–November (harvest flush). Farther origins (Twin Cities, Mid-Mississippi) carry higher average rates.

Figure 3-3 Correlation Matrix

Diesel (r=0.46) and Thebes gauge (r=-0.37) show the strongest linear correlations with barge rates. The negative gauge correlation reflects that lower water means higher rates.

3.3 River Gauge & Rate Relationship

Figure 3-4 Thebes Gauge Height vs St. Louis Barge Rates

Thebes gauge height (inverted right axis) overlaid with St. Louis barge rates. Low-water events clearly correspond to rate spikes, especially the 2022 and 2012 episodes.

3.4 Case Study: 2022 Low-Water Crisis

Figure 3-5 Rate Surge vs Gauge Collapse

The Thebes gauge dropped from approximately 30 feet in June to 5 feet by October 2022, coinciding with rates spiking to 2,800% of tariff. All origins were affected simultaneously — a system-wide event.

{# ===================================================== #}

4. Forecast Accuracy

4.1 Overall Walk-Forward Metrics

Evaluated on 335 (year, month, origin) predictions from 2010 through 2025. Training window expands each year — no look-ahead bias.

Table 4-1 Error Metrics (Walk-Forward, 2010–2025)

ModelMAERMSEMAPECorrelation
Forward Rate (benchmark)142.1240.322.9%0.61
ElasticNetCV (our model)136.0230.822.1%0.63

The model achieves 4.3% MAE improvement over the forward rate benchmark, with higher correlation and lower RMSE.

4.2 Forward Quote vs Actual Harvest Rate

Before comparing the model, we examine how well the market's own forward quote has tracked actual realised rates. This motivates why beating the forward rate is a meaningful benchmark.

Figure 4-1 Annual Average: Forward vs Actual (All Origins)

Upper panel: three-month forward rate (grey) quoted in June/July/August vs actual harvest-season rate (blue) in September/October/November. Lower panel: forecast error — red indicates the forward rate under-predicted, green indicates over-prediction. The forward market badly missed 2005, 2022, and 2020–2024, while over-predicting 2011 and 2015–2016.

Figure 4-2 St. Louis Monthly: Forward vs Actual (2004–2025)

Each point represents one harvest month at St. Louis. Red shading indicates actual rates exceeded the forward quote; green indicates the forward overshot. The 2022 October quote was approximately 750% of tariff while the actual reached 2,250% — the single worst forward-market miss on record.

Figure 4-3 Annual MAE Comparison

Figure 4-4 Predicted vs Actual Scatter

Our model (right) shows tighter clustering around the 45-degree line than the forward rate (left). Both struggle with the 2022 extreme values.

{# ===================================================== #}

5. Explanatory Model: Rate Sensitivity

5.1 Regression Coefficients

To quantify how sensitive barge rates are to each driver, we fit an OLS regression using contemporaneous (same-week) data — not lagged. This is not a forecasting model; it answers: “holding all else equal, how much does the rate change when variable X moves by one unit?” The model controls for seasonality (month dummies) and origin (location dummies) so each coefficient isolates that variable's independent effect.

Methodology

Specification: Linear regression of weekly St. Louis-benchmarked barge rates on contemporaneous predictors plus month and origin fixed effects. Robust (HC1) standard errors. Log-linear version run in parallel for percentage-effect interpretation. Standardised version for relative-importance comparison across variables.

Sample: 5,230 weekly observations from 2004–2026 across seven origins, after dropping rows with missing predictors.

Table 5-1 OLS Coefficient Estimates

VariableCoef (per unit)Std ErrSig % Effect / unitStd CoefMeanStd Dev
Thebes Gauge (ft)-0.181.04+0.21%-1.620.079.26
St. Louis Gauge (ft)-1.000.89-0.44%-10.312.7210.3
Baton Rouge Gauge (ft)+0.840.29***+0.20%+9.221.1810.95
Diesel Price ($/gal)+131.2313.62***+30.39%+97.73.390.74
USD/BRL-7.951.63***-1.91%-11.33.731.42
Gulf Inspections (M MT)+62.019.77***+27.08%+26.41.10.43
Shuttle Train Index+3.750.24***+0.55%+68.8104.4318.34
Truck Cost Index-1.000.33***-0.13%-28.2130.2428.16

Coef (per unit): change in barge rate (tariff points) per 1-unit increase, holding all else constant. % Effect: from the log-linear specification. Std Coef: effect per 1-standard-deviation change (for comparing relative importance across variables). Significance: *** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.10.

Figure 5-1 Relative Importance (Standardised)

Coefficients scaled to 1-standard-deviation units so magnitudes are directly comparable. Diesel price dominates, followed by Gulf inspections, shuttle train cost, and Baton Rouge gauge.

5.2 Interpreting Each Variable

Summary

  • Diesel is the dominant cost driver — a direct pass-through of operating costs into freight pricing.
  • Gulf inspections capture demand pressure — more grain moving to the Gulf means more barges needed.
  • Rail and truck costs set competitive floors: when alternative modes get expensive, barge rates can rise alongside.
  • River gauges quantify the physical constraint — low water restricts drafts and reduces effective barge capacity.
  • USD/BRL reflects trade competitiveness — a stronger dollar reduces US export demand and, by extension, barge traffic.
Thebes Gauge (ft) (coef: -0.18 )
Each 1-foot rise in the Thebes gauge reduces barge rates by about 0.2 tariff points (+0.2%). A 10-foot drop (drought conditions) would raise rates by ~2 points.
St. Louis Gauge (ft) (coef: -1.00 )
Each 1-foot rise in the St. Louis gauge reduces rates by 1.0 points (-0.4%). St. Louis gauge captures mid-Mississippi water conditions.
Baton Rouge Gauge (ft) (coef: +0.84 ***)
Each 1-foot rise in the Baton Rouge gauge reduces rates by 0.8 points (+0.2%). Baton Rouge is the final stretch to Gulf elevators.
Diesel Price ($/gal) (coef: +131.23 ***)
A $1/gallon increase in Midwest diesel raises barge rates by 131 tariff points (+30.4%). Diesel is ~30-40% of operating costs, so this captures the direct cost pass-through.
USD/BRL (coef: -7.95 ***)
A 1 BRL/USD move (dollar strengthening) changes rates by 8.0 points (-1.9%). A stronger dollar makes US exports less competitive, reducing Gulf demand and barge traffic.
Gulf Inspections (M MT) (coef: +62.01 ***)
Each additional million metric tons of weekly Gulf corn+soy inspections raises rates by 62 tariff points (+27.1%). More grain moving to the Gulf = more barges needed.
Shuttle Train Index (coef: +3.75 ***)
A 1-point rise in the shuttle train cost index raises barge rates by 3.8 points (+0.6%). When rail gets more expensive, shippers shift to barges.
Truck Cost Index (coef: -1.00 ***)
A 1-point rise in the truck cost index raises barge rates by 1.0 points (-0.1%). Truck and barge compete for short-haul grain movements to river elevators.

5.3 Scenario Sensitivity

Figure 5-2 Rate vs Thebes Gauge Height

Predicted rate at different Thebes gauge levels with all other variables at their historical means. The relationship is approximately linear — each foot matters about the same in this specification.

Figure 5-3 Rate vs Diesel Price

A move from $2.50 to $5.00 diesel would raise predicted rates by roughly 330 tariff points, nearly doubling the average rate.

Figure 5-4 Scenario Analysis: Rates Under Stress Conditions

Each bar shows the predicted barge rate under a specific scenario, with all other variables at their historical means. The final "Crisis" bar combines drought (Thebes at 8 ft) with high diesel ($5.50/gal) — conditions similar to late 2022.

{# ===================================================== #}

6. Forecast Model Coefficients

The forecasting model (ElasticNetCV, α=0.0459, L1 ratio=0.1) is fitted on log-transformed harvest rates with standardised features. Below are the coefficients from the final model trained on all available data.

25 of 42 features have non-zero coefficients (L1 regularisation zeroed out the rest). Effect sizes are evaluated at the median harvest rate of 509.7% of tariff.

{# ===================================================== #}

7. Key Findings

  1. The forward rate is a strong but beatable benchmark. Using it as a feature (rather than ignoring it) is the single biggest model improvement — the model learns where the market is systematically wrong.
  2. River gauge departures from normal are the most valuable physical signal. Summer drought conditions (captured by Thebes gauge departure) predict fall rate spikes that the forward market underweights.
  3. Linear models outperform tree-based models at this sample size. With approximately 460 training examples, LightGBM overfits despite heavy regularisation. ElasticNet with log-transform is the right complexity level.
  4. Tail events remain the primary challenge. The 2022 low-water crisis produced rates 5–8× normal levels. Our model reduced the 2022 error (MAE 581 vs 638 for the forward rate) but still under-predicted the magnitude of the spike.
  5. Competitive transport pricing adds value. Shuttle train cost indices and diesel prices capture the cost floor that barge rates must clear to attract traffic away from rail.
  6. The explanatory model confirms physical intuitions. Low gauges → higher rates; high diesel → higher rates; expensive rail → higher rates; strong dollar → lower rates via reduced export competitiveness.
Data sources: USDA AMS Grain Transportation Report · USGS Water Services · U.S. Energy Information Administration · Federal Reserve Economic Data · USDA NASS QuickStats
Generated April 19, 2026 at 20:48 · Walk-forward validation 2010–2025 · barge-freight-model
